data.title: Air Force One to land at Joint Base Andrews after crew finds 'minor electrical issue'
data.description:
Air Force One on Tuesday, en route to the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, will land at Joint Base Andrews after crew members identified “a minor electrical issue.”

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed that the issue was found after takeoff and, out of an abundance of caution, Air Force One will turn around and land at the base before taking off in a different aircraft.

Leavitt joked that the Qatari jet given to the U.S. to replace Air Force One in May was sounding “much better” now.

Trump accepted the gift jet, a $400 million luxury Boeing 747-8 that the Qatari royal family had previously used. Chief Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell previously said the Department of Defense will “work to ensure proper security measures and functional-mission requirements are considered for an aircraft used to transport the President of the United States.”

The president and his Cabinet are expected to meet with European leaders in Davos amid Trump’s escalating rhetoric over the U.S. acquiring Greenland. On Saturday, Trump slapped 10 percent tariffs on Denmark and other European allies until the U.S. acquires the island territory.

data.title: Bondi announces departure of Halligan from US Attorneys office
data.description:
Attorney General Pam Bondi on Tuesday announced that Lindsey Halligan, the top prosecutor in the Eastern District of Virginia, has departed her position, citing Senate Democrats “making it impossible” for her to continue past her 120-day appointment deadline.

“During her 120-day tenure as Interim United States Attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia, Lindsey Halligan served with the utmost distinction and an unwavering commitment to the rule of law,” Bondi said in a statement posted on the social platform X.

Bondi accused Senate Democrats of weaponizing the “blue slip process, making it impossible for Lindsey’s term as United States Attorney to continue following the expiration of her 120-day appointment.”

Halligan’s departure “is a significant loss for the Department of Justice and the communities she served,” Bondi said. “While we feel her absence keenly, we are confident that she will continue to serve her country in other ways.”

“The circumstances that led to this outcome are deeply misguided,” Bondi continued in closing. “We are living in a time when a democratically elected President’s ability to staff key law enforcement positions faces serious obstacles. The Department of Justice will continue to seek review of decisions like this that hinder our ability to keep the American people safe.”

Earlier on Tuesday, Chief District Judge M. Hannah Lauck posted a job opening for interim U.S. attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia. He entered an order stating that Halligan “may not serve” in her previous role for more than 120 days.

Bondi tapped Halligan after President Trump’s previous U.S. attorney nominee Erik Siebert refused to investigate New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) in connection with mortgage fraud. Halligan launched inquiries into James and former FBI Director James Comey regarding allegations that they lied during congressional testimony.

Halligan defended her position after U.S. District Judge Cameron Currie ruled in November that she was ineligible to stay in her role and dismissed the cases against James and Comey. Currie argued that her appointment expired while Siebert was U.S. attorney and ruled that she was not eligible to be the district’s top federal prosecutor.

U.S. District Judge David Novak ordered Halligan on Jan. 6 to give an explanation of why she continued her duties despite Currie’s order.

“The bottom line is that Ms. Halligan has not ‘misrepresented’ anything and the Court is flat wrong to suggest that any change to the Government’s signature block is warranted in this or any other case,” an 11-page filing to Novak read.

Halligan was one of several of Trump’s U.S. attorneys to be disqualified, with the top prosecutors in New Jersey, the Northern District of New York, Nevada and the district in California covering Los Angeles all having been disqualified. A challenge is pending against a prosecutor in New Mexico.

data.title: Trump: Anything Europe does to threaten US on trade 'I'll just meet it'
data.description:
President Trump said Tuesday in an interview with The Hill’s sister network NewsNation that he will match Europe if it moves to threaten the U.S. on trade amid rising tensions over Greenland. 

“See anything they do with us, I’ll just meet it. All I have to do is meet it, and it’s going to go ricocheting backward,” Trump told NewsNation’s Katie Pavlich on “Katie Pavlich Tonight.” 

“But we’re not looking at that. We’ll probably be able to work something out, possibly even during the next few days in Davos,” he continued.

The interview comes as Trump travels to Davos, Switzerland for the World Economic Forum where he’ll come face to face with a number of European allies who have raised concerns of his vocal ambitions for the U.S. to acquire Greenland. 

Over the weekend, Trump levied 10 percent tariffs on Denmark and its European allies over Greenland. Additionally, the president has not ruled out military force to acquire the territory. 

UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer issued a rare criticism of Trump over the issue, saying at a press conference earlier that “any decision about the future status of Greenland belongs to the people of Greenland and the kingdom of Denmark alone.”

Trump on Tuesday also shared a screenshot of a conversation with French President Emmanuel Macron in which Macron tells him, “I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland.” 

However, Trump appeared optimistic at Tuesday’s press briefing, predicting a deal would be made on Greenland at Davos. 

“I think we’re going to work something out where NATO’s going to be very happy and we’re going to be very happy,” Trump said.

data.title: Trump signs executive order barring Wall Street investors from buying single-family homes
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday signed an executive order stopping Wall Street investors from buying and owning single-family homes, amid his push to focus on affordability ahead of the November midterm elections.

“Buying and owning a home has long been considered the pinnacle of the American dream and a way for families to invest and build lifetime wealth,” Trump’s order reads. “But because of the recent high inflation and interest rates caused by the previous administration, that American dream has been increasingly out of reach for too many of our citizens, especially first-time homebuyers.”

Trump said “large” Wall Street investors have bought a “growing share of single-family homes,” creating competition with “hardworking young families.

“Neighborhoods and communities once controlled by middle-class American families are now run by faraway corporate interests,” Trump continued. “People live in homes, not corporations.  My Administration will take decisive action to stop Wall Street from treating America’s neighborhoods like a trading floor and empower American families to own their homes.”

The order stated that in 30 days, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent will develop “definitions of ‘large institutional investor’ and ‘single-family home.'” Cabinet members will issue guidance to prevent “providing for, approving, insuring, guaranteeing, securitizing, or facilitating the acquisition by large institutional investors” of a single-family home.

Attorney General Pam Bondi and Federal Trade Commission Chair Andrew Ferguson will review substantial acquisitions by heavy investors “and prioritize enforcement of the antitrust laws, as appropriate, against coordinated vacancy and pricing strategies by large institutional investors in local single-family home rental markets,” the order reads.

Trump called for the ban earlier this month, writing on Truth Social that he was “taking steps” to implement it, adding that he “will be calling on Congress to codify it.”

“For a very long time, buying and owning a home was considered the pinnacle of the American Dream,” he wrote. “It was the reward for working hard, and doing the right thing, but now, because of the Record High Inflation caused by Joe Biden and the Democrats in Congress, that American Dream is increasingly out of reach for far too many people, especially younger Americans.”

Many real estate trusts and private equity firms have collected a considerable number of single-family homes ultimately used for rent, destabilizing the homebuying market.

Trump and Republicans in Congress have focused on affordability ahead of the midterms to tell voters that inflation has fallen and prices have improved. Americans largely do not believe conditions have improved, which Trump acknowledged on Tuesday that the administration might have “bad public relations people” selling his economic achievements.

data.title: Healthcare group projects anti-RFK Jr. messages onto HHS building
data.description:
Protect Our Care, a healthcare advocacy group, on Tuesday projected two critical messages onto the facade of the Health and Human Services (HHS) building in Washington, D.C., blaming HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. for policies they say are making Americans sicker.

“RFK JR: MAKING AMERICA SICK AGAIN,” read one message, projected outside the building.

Another message read: “MEASLES HQ.”

Kayla Hancock, director of Public Health Watch at Protect Our Care, told The Hill that the messages aim to raise awareness of the resurgence of Measles and to push the Trump administration to act with greater urgency to encourage vaccination.

 “Secretary Kennedy has been busy doing anything and everything but addressing or even acknowledging the worsening measles outbreak that is almost exclusively harming unvaccinated children,” Hancock said.

“We hope that shining a literal spotlight on the HHS will spur the Trump administration to raise awareness of the proven safety and efficacy of the measles vaccine,” she continued. “It’s the least RFK Jr. can do after he helped cause this mess by spreading dangerous, fabricated lies about the shot that likely scared many parents out of vaccinating their children.”

The messages on the HHS building came the same day the U.S. met a key condition required to lose its measles elimination status, which the country has had for more than 25 years.

The Pan American Health Organization (PAHO), the World Health Organization’s (WHO) regional office for the Americas, considers Jan. 20, 2025, the date marking the beginning of consistent measles spread in the U.S. 

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), however, said it’s too soon to declare an end to the country’s measles elimination status, noting that the exact chain of transmission must first be determined.

Ralph Abraham, a former Louisiana surgeon general who was sworn in as the CDC’s principal deputy director last month, said it’s unclear whether the West Texas outbreak in January is related to the ongoing spread in states such as South Carolina, which has reported more than 600 cases since October.  

Protect Our Care held a virtual news conference earlier Tuesday, when Hawaii Gov. Josh Green (D), who is also a physician, said the Trump administration “laid the foundation for mistrust and distrust of vaccinations over the course of this year, and … now you’re seeing the terrible outcomes.”

“What’s happening today with Measles is absolutely public health malpractice, and it’s completely preventable,” Green said earlier Tuesday.

“It’s unconscionable that HHS is allowing this to happen,” he added. “I don’t criticize them for everything that they do, but this, more than anything, is a tragedy, because it’s completely, completely preventable.”

data.title: Georgia state Senate race heads to runoff
data.description:
The race to fill a state Senate seat in Georgia is headed to a runoff, Decision Desk HQ has projected.

Democrat Lemario Brown and Republican Steven McNeel will head to a Feb. 17 runoff after neither candidate was able to cross the 50 percent threshold. There were five Republicans running, splitting the vote and helping Brown advance in a reliably red district.

The Peach State’s 18th state Senate district became vacant when the incumbent, Republican state Sen. John F. Kennedy (R), resigned last month to focus on his campaign for lieutenant governor, triggering a special election.

Republicans are favored to win the seat, where Kennedy won reelection uncontested in 2024.

Republicans hold a 32-23 majority in the Georgia state Senate, which gaveled in this month and is expected to wrap up in late March or early April, according to the Georgia Recorder. 

The race comes after two special elections for the state legislature in the last two months headed to runoffs. A Democrat won a Georgia Senate seat in December, and a Republican won a Georgia House seat in January.

Democrats also flipped two seats on the Georgia Public Service Commission and flipped a state House seat in a red-leaning district last year, adding to blue momentum ahead of the midterms. 

The winner of the special election would have to run again next fall to win a full term in the state chamber.

data.title: Home sellers outnumber buyers by widest margin on record, Redfin says
data.description:
(NewsNation) —  A few years ago, buyers were battling for homes. Now, they’re the ones with leverage.

In December, there were 47% more home sellers than buyers — roughly 631,000 extra — the largest gap in records going back to 2013, according to new estimates from Redfin.

The widening gap reflects a sharp drop in the number of active buyers, which fell to 1.34 million last month, the lowest level in Redfin’s 12 years of data. Buyers haven’t been this scarce since April 2020 at the start of the pandemic.

“Homebuyers are backing off due to stubbornly high home prices and mortgage rates, layoffs, and mounting economic and political uncertainty,” Redfin noted.

It’s a stark reversal from 2021, when bidding wars were routine and buyers outnumbered sellers. The two sides were roughly even as recently as Feb. 2024, but since then, the number of sellers has consistently outpaced buyers.

The imbalance is most visible in the Sun Belt, where pandemic boomtowns fueled a wave of new construction that added supply and helped slow price growth. Demand has also cooled after years of steep price gains.

In Austin, there were an estimated 128% more home sellers than buyers in December — the widest gap among the top 50 metros. Fort Lauderdale (125%), Nashville (111%), Miami (103%) and San Antonio (103%) also posted large imbalances, Redfin said.

Part of the challenge for sellers is that many who bought near the peak of the pandemic market have been slow to reset their expectations.

Connie Durnal, a Redfin Premier agent in the Dallas area, said some clients are adjusting to the new reality, while others are not: “A lot of sellers are in denial and won’t budge on price.”

“If you don’t price your home reasonably, it will sit on the market,” Durnal warned in the report.

In the Northeast and Midwest — regions that have historically issued the fewest building permits — the picture looks different. Sellers continue to have the upper hand and prices are rising faster than the national average in several markets.

Buyers outnumber sellers by the widest margin in Nassau County, NY, followed by Montgomery County, PA and Newark, NJ. Milwaukee, WI, and New Brunswick, N,J also had more buyers than sellers last month.

Overall, 36 of the 50 most populous metros were considered buyer’s markets in December, meaning they had at least 10% more sellers than buyers, Redfin said. By comparison, just give metros qualified as sellers’ markets.

The growing divide between buyers and sellers underscores how weak the housing market remained in 2025, with existing-home sales hovering near a 30-year low.

Long-term mortgage rates have eased recently, hitting their lowest level in more than three years, but at 6.06% the average 30-year fixed is still roughly double where it stood in 2021.

Forecasters don’t expect mortgage rates to fall much further in 2026, despite actions by President Donald Trump aimed at lowering borrowing costs.

The good news for prospective buyers is that the so-called “lock-in effect” has been easing, with the share of homeowners holding sub-3% rates declining. That shift could help improve inventory in the months and years ahead.

Redfin has dubbed 2026 “The Great Housing Reset,” calling it the start of a “long, slow recovery” in a recent forecast.

Redfin estimates the number of active buyers using proprietary data on the typical time between a buyer’s first home tour and closing, along with MLS data on active listings and pending sales.

Top 10 buyer’s markets in the U.S. in Dec. 2025 (Redfin data for top 50 metros)

Percent by which sellers outnumber buyers

  1. Austin, TX: 128.4%
  2. Fort Lauderdale, FL: 125.1%
  3. Nashville, TN: 111.3%
  4. Miami, FL: 102.7%
  5. San Antonio, TX: 102.5%
  6. West Palm Beach, FL: 97.4%
  7. Houston, TX: 96.6%
  8. Las Vegas, NV: 88.9%
  9. Dallas, TX: 86.8%
  10. Tampa, FL: 82.2%

data.title: Lutnick not expecting 'upending' of existing trade deals: 'It'll sort its way out'
data.description:
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick dismissed concerns that President Trump’s new tariffs could upend existing trade deals, saying he’s confident the disagreement with European allies over Trump’s push to acquire Greenland will “sort its way out.”

In an interview with CBS News on Tuesday at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, Lutnick defended Trump’s approach to Greenland and touted the durability of the trade deals struck with the European Union and other allies.

“I do not think that there is going to be an upending of any of the trade deals that we have made,” Lutnick told CBS News’s Ed O’Keefe.

“There are things that we will argue about between our two countries, for sure, because that’s the way it works, with friends and allies,” Lutnick said, referring to the U.S. and EU. “But I would say Donald J. Trump understands what he wants, and he will talk to them, and they will work it out.”

“And I think the EU trade deal is solid, firm and will continue,” he added.

Trump has faced strong resistance from European allies to his push to take control of Greenland, a Danish territory. He responded this weekend by unveiling new tariffs on Denmark, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, Germany, Norway, Sweden, France and Finland. Trump said 10 percent tariffs would take effect on Feb. 1, rising to 25 percent on June 1.

The president wrote on Truth Social that the tariffs “will be due and payable until such time as a Deal is reached for the Complete and Total purchase of Greenland.”

Pushback has been fierce, with European allies saying the trade deal struck last summer with the U.S. “must be put on hold” because of “Trump’s threats regarding Greenland.”

“They haven’t thought about it at all. I think they will think about it more, and they will address it,” Lutnick said about European resistance to the U.S. taking control of Greenland.

“You can have disagreements with your friends and allies. You can. And then eventually it’ll sort its way out,” the Commerce secretary said, noting, “I’m going to leave that to my national security people to address it with the Europeans.”

data.title: Former Trump press secretary on marathon briefing: 'Feels like he's…mentally slipping'
data.description:
Former White House press secretary Stephanie Grisham on Tuesday commentated during President Trump’s marathon press briefing, saying it feels “like he’s…mentally slipping.”

“This presser is bizarre even for him,” Grisham posted on the social platform X. “It’s all the usual rambling, off-topic tales, half-truths, lies, ‘I’ve fixed everything – no one has ever seen anything like it’ stuff…but it’s low-energy & feels like he’s…mentally slipping. Congress-plz wake up. Plz. #EmperorHasNoClothes.”

Grisham, who also served as an aide to first lady Melania Trump during the first Trump administration, resigned from the first Trump administration following the Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol attack. She has become an outspoken critic of the president in the years since.

She was among other anti-Trump Republicans asked to speak at the Democratic National Convention in August 2024, weeks after endorsing former Vice President Kamala Harris. Grisham took to the stage and said she was once a “true believer” in Trump.

“Behind closed doors, Trump mocks his supporters,” she said. “He calls them ‘basement dwellers.’ He has no empathy, no morals and no fidelity to the truth.”

Grisham’s comments come after Dr. Jonathan Reiner, cardiologist to the late former Vice President Dick Cheney, called for a congressional inquiry into Trump’s presidential fitness. Reiner cited a letter Trump sent to Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre, where the president appeared to link his threats to acquire Greenland to not receiving the Nobel Peace Prize after he openly campaigned for it.

“This letter, and the fact that the president directed that it be distributed to other European countries, should trigger a bipartisan congressional inquiry into presidential fitness,” Reiner wrote on X. 

Democratic lawmakers have called for the 25th Amendment to be invoked to remove Trump from office. Rep. Yassamin Ansari (D-Ariz.), in sharing Trump’s letter to Støre in an X post, said the 25th Amendment “exists for a reason—we need to invoke it immediately.”

“Invoke the 25th Amendment,” Sen. Ed Markey (D-Mass.) wrote on X, sharing an image of a New York Times article reporting on Trump’s letter to Støre.

On Tuesday Trump joined current White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt in a press briefing to mark one full year into his second term. The president addressed a myriad of issues from federal immigration enforcement in Minnesota to his growing calls for the U.S. to acquire Greenland. He praised his administration’s work over the past year and, on the economy, said public is not seeing his economic accomplishments.

Trump posted on Truth Social earlier this month that he was in “perfect health” and that he “‘ACED’ (Meaning, was correct on 100% of the questions asked!), for the third straight time, my Cognitive Examination, something which no other President, or previous Vice President, was willing to take.”

data.title: Democrats win another Virginia state House special election
data.description:
Virginia voters gave Democrats another special election win on Tuesday, according to Decision Desk HQ, adding to a string of recent victories for the party in the Old Dominion.

Democrat Garrett McGuire will replace state Del. Mark Sickles (D), who has been tapped to be Gov. Abigail Spanberger’s secretary of finance, in the 17th House District.

The Tuesday contests are part of a series of special elections prompted as Spanberger, who was elected governor last year and inaugurated over the weekend, called on several state lawmakers to join her administration.

Democrats were expected to win in the district, which went to former Vice President Kamala Harris by 43 points in 2024. 

Democrats have the majority in the House of Delegates, where state lawmakers moved forward quickly this month with constitutional amendments on abortion access, mid-decade redistricting and other major issues.

Another special election is slated for Feb. 10, as State Sen. Adam Ebbin is also resigning from the Virginia Senate — where Democrats have a tighter margin — to serve in Spanberger’s administration as senior adviser at Virginia’s Cannabis Control Authority.
